subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Assalamu alaikum, and welcome to one of the most exciting units in your science year. In this unit you will study the physics of the world you live in every single day, from the tiniest trembling of the air to the enormous movements of the whole planet. We call this Earth physics because it connects three big ideas: how sound is made and travels, what the Earth is made of on the inside, and how the Sun, Moon and Earth move together to create eclipses. These may sound like three separate topics, but they are all about the same thing: how energy and matter behave in the world around you.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 7 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Explain that sound is made by vibrations and describe how those vibrations start.
Describe how a sound wave travels by making particles vibrate backwards and forwards.
State that sound can travel through solids, liquids and gases, but not through a vacuum, and explain why.
Describe how sound waves are reflected to make echoes, and give useful and unwanted examples.
Distinguish between loudness (volume) and pitch, and connect each to the vibration.
Describe the structure of the Earth: crust, mantle, outer core and inner core.
Explain the evidence for continental drift and describe tectonic plates.
Explain how fold mountains, volcanoes and earthquakes form at plate boundaries.
Explain how the Sun, Moon and Earth produce solar and lunar eclipses.
State clearly why you must never look directly at the Sun, even during an eclipse.

concepts.title: How Sound Is Made
concepts.explanation: Let us begin with something you have heard every day of your life: sound. Close your eyes for a moment and imagine the sounds around you right now. Perhaps you can hear voices, a goat bleating outside, the wind moving through an acacia tree, or a cooking pot being placed on the fire. Every one of these sounds started in exactly the same way: something vibrated. To vibrate means to move backwards and forwards very quickly. When something vibrates, it pushes against the air around it and makes a sound. Think of the…
concepts.example: You can feel a vibration in your own body. Place two fingers gently on the front of your throat and say your name out loud, or softly recite 'Bismillah'. Can you feel the gentle buzzing? That buzzing comes from your…

concepts.title: How a Sound Wave Travels
concepts.explanation: Now we come to a deeper question. When the muadhin calls the adhan at the mosque, how does that sound reach your house far away? The vibration happens at the mosque, but you hear it in your home. Something must carry the sound across the distance. That something is a sound wave. Here is how it works, step by step. When an object vibrates, it moves forwards and pushes the air particles in front of it. Those particles get squeezed together for a moment. Then the object moves back, and the particles spread out again.…
concepts.example: This is a very important idea, so let us be clear about it. The particles themselves do not travel all the way from the mosque to your house. The air does not fly across the town. Instead, each particle nudges the next…

concepts.title: Echoes: Sound That Bounces Back
concepts.explanation: One special property of all waves is that they can be reflected, which means they bounce off a surface, just as a football bounces back off a wall. Because sound is a wave, sound can be reflected too. A reflected sound wave has a special name: an echo. Sound reflects best from large, smooth, hard, flat surfaces such as a concrete wall, a cliff face, tiles or glass. If you stand in a big empty room with bare walls and clap your hands once, the sound seems to last longer and then fade, because the clap keeps…
concepts.example: Echoes can be very useful. Bats hunting insects at dusk send out sounds and listen for the echo bouncing off an insect; from the time the echo takes to return, the bat works out exactly where its food is. Fishing boats…

concepts.title: Inside the Earth
concepts.explanation: We now leave sound behind and travel downwards, deep into the ground beneath your feet. Have you ever wondered what is inside the Earth? You cannot dig down to find out, because the centre of the Earth is far too deep and far too hot. Yet scientists have worked out what the inside of the Earth is like, and they have also worked out that the Earth is about 4500 million years old, which is an almost unimaginable length of time. If you could slice the Earth in half like a giant fruit, you would see that it is made of…
concepts.example: The crust is the thin outer layer of solid rock. This is the ground you walk on, farm, and build your home upon, whether in Garissa, Dar es Salaam or Lamu. Compared with the whole Earth, the crust is very thin, like the…

concepts.title: When the Earth Changes: Mountains, Volcanoes and Earthquakes
concepts.explanation: The places where two tectonic plates meet are called plate boundaries, and these are the most exciting and sometimes the most dangerous places on Earth. Because the plates are always moving, most big geological changes happen at plate boundaries. Some changes are very slow, taking millions of years, while others are sudden and violent. There are three main things plates can do when they meet. When two plates push together, one plate can be forced down underneath the other. This is called subduction. The rock that…
concepts.example: When two plates pull apart, the crust cracks and stretches until it becomes too thin. Hot molten rock from the mantle rises up through the cracks and erupts at the surface as lava, which cools and hardens into new rock.…

concepts.title: The Sun, the Moon and Eclipses
concepts.explanation: Now we look up. The Sun is a star that gives out its own light. The Moon does not give out any light of its own; we only see the Moon because sunlight bounces off it. Both the Earth and the Moon are opaque, which means light cannot pass through them. Whenever an opaque object blocks light, it casts a shadow, an area where the light has been blocked. Light is important to understand here, so remember one rule: light travels in straight lines, which we call rays. Because light travels in straight lines and cannot…
concepts.example: Solar Eclipse - the Moon's Shadow Falls on Earth

explorations.title: The Ruler and the Rubber Band: Making Sound
explorations.context: To show that sound is made by vibrations, and to explore what changes the loudness and the pitch of a sound.
explorations.prompt: Before you start, write down what you think. If I make the vibrating part longer, then the pitch will get... (higher or lower?) and if I pluck harder, then the sound will be... (louder or quieter?). Write your predictions so you can check them afterwards.
explorations.answer: When did you get a higher pitch: with a long or a short vibrating part? What did you have to do to make the sound louder? What is actually making the sound in each case? Did a tighter rubber band give a higher or a lower pitch?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To show that sound is made by vibrations, and to explore what changes the loudness and the pitch of a sound.

explorations.title: The String Telephone: Sound Through a Solid
explorations.context: To show that sound needs vibrations and a medium to travel, and that sound can travel well through a solid string.
explorations.prompt: Predict: When the string is pulled tight, I think the voice will be... (clear or not clear?). When the string is loose, I think it will be... Write your prediction before testing.
explorations.answer: What did the sound wave travel through to get from one cup to the other? Why was the voice clearer when the string was tight than when it was slack? What happened when someone pinched the string, and why? What does this tell you about whether sound needs a medium to travel?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To show that sound needs vibrations and a medium to travel, and that sound can travel well through a solid string.

explorations.title: Hunting for Echoes
explorations.context: To find out which places and surfaces produce the clearest echoes, and to understand reflection of sound.
explorations.prompt: Predict which will give a stronger echo: a bare room with hard walls, or a room full of soft cushions and cloth? Write down why you think so.
explorations.answer: Which place gave the strongest echo? Which gave almost none? What kind of surface reflects sound best: hard and flat, or soft and uneven? Why do soft cushions and curtains reduce echoes? How could a musician use this knowledge when choosing a room to record in?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out which places and surfaces produce the clearest echoes, and to understand reflection of sound.

explorations.title: The Ball-and-Torch Eclipse Model
explorations.context: To model how solar and lunar eclipses happen using a torch and two balls of different sizes.
explorations.prompt: Predict: For a solar eclipse, which ball must go in the middle, and whose shadow falls on whom? Write your prediction, then test it with the model.
explorations.answer: In a solar eclipse, which object is in the middle, and whose shadow lands where? In a lunar eclipse, which object is in the middle, and whose shadow lands where? Why can you only see a solar eclipse in the daytime and a lunar eclipse at night? Why do we not get an eclipse every month, even though…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To model how solar and lunar eclipses happen using a torch and two balls of different sizes.

methods.title: The Ruler and the Rubber Band: Making Sound
methods.example: To show that sound is made by vibrations, and to explore what changes the loudness and the pitch of a sound.
steps: Hold the ruler firmly on the table so that most of it sticks out over the edge.
Twang the free end so it vibrates. Watch it move and listen to the sound.
Now slide the ruler so that only a short part sticks out. Twang it again.
Repeat with a long part sticking out. Listen carefully to how the pitch changes.
Next, stretch the rubber band between your fingers, or over an empty tin, and pluck it gently, then pluck it harder. Listen to the loudness.

methods.title: The String Telephone: Sound Through a Solid
methods.example: To show that sound needs vibrations and a medium to travel, and that sound can travel well through a solid string.
steps: Carefully make a small hole in the bottom of each cup with the sharp point. Push away from your hand and ask an adult to help.
Thread the string through both holes so the open ends of the cups face away from each other.
Tie a knot inside each cup so the string cannot slip out.
Give one cup to your partner and walk apart until the string is pulled tight.
One person speaks quietly into their cup while the other holds their cup over their ear and listens.

methods.title: Hunting for Echoes
methods.example: To find out which places and surfaces produce the clearest echoes, and to understand reflection of sound.
steps: Stand in an empty room with hard, bare walls. Clap once, sharply. Listen for the sound lasting or bouncing.
Move to a room with soft furniture, curtains or cushions. Clap the same way and listen.
Go outside into an open space with no walls nearby. Clap and listen.
If it is safe, stand a good distance from a large flat wall and clap or shout, listening for the echo returning.
Record how strong the echo was in each place.

methods.title: The Ball-and-Torch Eclipse Model
methods.example: To model how solar and lunar eclipses happen using a torch and two balls of different sizes.
steps: Darken the room. Place the large ball (Earth) on a cup or bowl so it does not roll.
Shine the torch (Sun) at the Earth from about half a metre away. Notice one side is lit (day) and one side is dark (night).
Solar eclipse: hold the small ball (Moon) between the torch and the Earth. Look for the small shadow of the Moon falling on the Earth.
Move the Moon slightly and watch the shadow move across the surface of the Earth.
Lunar eclipse: now move the Moon to the far side of the Earth, into the Earth's shadow. Watch the Earth's shadow fall on the Moon.

workedExamples.title: Timing an Echo
workedExamples.prompt: Some questions about sound involve a calculation. Let us work through one carefully, showing every step, so you can follow the method.
workedExamples.solution: Problem: Halima stands in a valley and shouts towards a tall cliff. She hears the echo return exactly 2 seconds later. Sound travels at about 343 metres per second in air. How far away is the cliff? Step 1 - Find the total distance the sound travelled. We use the rule distance = speed x time. The sound travelled for 2 seconds at 343 metres per second, so distance = 343 x 2 = 686 metres. Step 2 - Remember the sound made a round trip. The 686 metres is the distance to the cliff and back again. To find the distance…

workedExamples.title: Guided example 2
workedExamples.prompt: A guitar makes a sound mainly because: A) it is made of wood B) its strings vibrate C) there is air inside D) it has metal parts
workedExamples.solution: 2 B It is the vibrating strings that make the note, not the wood or the metal parts.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: Sound travels through air as a: A) current B) stream C) wind D) wave
workedExamples.solution: 3 D Sound travels through air as a sound wave, passed particle to particle.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these can sound NOT travel through? A) solid B) liquid C) gas D) vacuum
workedExamples.solution: 4 D A vacuum has no particles, so there is nothing to vibrate and carry the sound.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: The pitch of a sound depends on: A) how big the vibration is B) how fast the object vibrates C) the colour of the object D) the temperature
workedExamples.solution: 5 B Pitch depends on how fast the object vibrates; faster vibration gives a higher pitch.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: A reflected sound wave is called: A) an echo B) a shadow C) a medium D) a ray
workedExamples.solution: 6 A A reflected sound wave is called an echo.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: Which surface gives the BEST echo? A) thick carpet B) soft curtain C) glass window D) wool blanket
workedExamples.solution: 7 C Hard, smooth, flat surfaces like glass reflect sound best; soft materials absorb it.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: The layers of the Earth from the outside inwards are: A) core, mantle, crust B) crust, mantle, core C) mantle, crust, core D) crust, core, mantle
workedExamples.solution: 8 B The correct order from outside to centre is crust, mantle, core.

practice.answer: 1 C A vibration is always needed to make a sound; heat, light and magnets do not make sound by themselves.

practice.prompt: The layers of the Earth from the outside inwards are: A) core, mantle, crust B) crust, mantle, core C) mantle, crust, core D) crust, core, mantle

practice.prompt: The inner core of the Earth is: A) solid metal B) liquid metal C) solid rock D) molten rock
practice.answer: 9 A The inner core is solid metal (iron and nickel), kept solid by huge pressure.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Tectonic plates move at about the speed that: A) a car drives B) your fingernails grow C) a person walks D) a river flows
practice.answer: 10 B Plates move only about 4 cm a year, about as fast as fingernails grow.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: An earthquake is caused when plates: A) pull apart B) stay perfectly still C) stick, build up pressure, then suddenly slip D) melt away
practice.answer: 11 C Plates stick due to friction, pressure builds, then they suddenly slip: an earthquake.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Molten rock on the surface of the Earth is called: A) magma B) mantle C) lava D) crust
practice.answer: 12 C Molten rock is called lava on the surface and magma underground.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: The Ruler and the Rubber Band: Making Sound
activities.materials: A plastic or wooden ruler (or a flat stick).; A sturdy table or desk edge.; A rubber band (an elastic band).; An empty tin, cup or small box to stretch the band over (optional).
steps: Hold the ruler firmly on the table so that most of it sticks out over the edge.
Twang the free end so it vibrates. Watch it move and listen to the sound.
Now slide the ruler so that only a short part sticks out. Twang it again.
Repeat with a long part sticking out. Listen carefully to how the pitch changes.
Next, stretch the rubber band between your fingers, or over an empty tin, and pluck it gently, then pluck it harder. Listen to the loudness.

activities.title: The String Telephone: Sound Through a Solid
activities.materials: Two empty tins, paper cups or plastic cups.; A long piece of string (as long as a room if possible).; A sharp point (a nail or pencil tip) to make a small hole - ask an adult.; A partner to talk to.
steps: Carefully make a small hole in the bottom of each cup with the sharp point. Push away from your hand and ask an adult to help.
Thread the string through both holes so the open ends of the cups face away from each other.
Tie a knot inside each cup so the string cannot slip out.
Give one cup to your partner and walk apart until the string is pulled tight.
One person speaks quietly into their cup while the other holds their cup over their ear and listens.

activities.title: Hunting for Echoes
activities.materials: Your own hands (for clapping) or your voice.; Access to different spaces: an empty room with bare walls, a room with curtains and cushions, an open outdoor space, and if possible a spot near a large wall or cliff.; A notebook to record what you hear.
steps: Stand in an empty room with hard, bare walls. Clap once, sharply. Listen for the sound lasting or bouncing.
Move to a room with soft furniture, curtains or cushions. Clap the same way and listen.
Go outside into an open space with no walls nearby. Clap and listen.
If it is safe, stand a good distance from a large flat wall and clap or shout, listening for the echo returning.
Record how strong the echo was in each place.

activities.title: The Ball-and-Torch Eclipse Model
activities.materials: A torch or a phone light (this is the Sun).; A large ball, such as a football or a large orange (this is the Earth).; A small ball, such as a tennis ball, a lime or a small stone (this is the Moon).; A dark room. Close the curtains or work at night.
steps: Darken the room. Place the large ball (Earth) on a cup or bowl so it does not roll.
Shine the torch (Sun) at the Earth from about half a metre away. Notice one side is lit (day) and one side is dark (night).
Solar eclipse: hold the small ball (Moon) between the torch and the Earth. Look for the small shadow of the Moon falling on the Earth.
Move the Moon slightly and watch the shadow move across the surface of the Earth.
Lunar eclipse: now move the Moon to the far side of the Earth, into the Earth's shadow. Watch the Earth's shadow fall on the Moon.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: To hear a sound you need TWO things: 1. Something vibrating to make the sound. 2. A medium of particles (solid, liquid or gas) for the wave to travel through. No vibration, or no medium, means no sound.

rules.title: Key idea 2
rules.text: Three plate movements, three results: Plates pushing together -> fold mountains (and subduction). Plates pulling apart -> volcanoes and new crust. Plates sliding past -> earthquakes. Magma is underground; lava is on the surface.

terms: Vibrate
To move backwards and forwards very quickly.
Sound wave
Vibrations of particles that carry sound energy from a source to your ear.
Medium
The substance (solid, liquid or gas) a wave travels through.
Particles
The tiny pieces that all substances are made from.
Vacuum
A space with no particles at all; sound cannot travel through it.
Pitch
How high or low a sound is; depends on how FAST something vibrates.
Loudness (volume)
How loud or quiet a sound is; depends on how BIG the vibration is.
Echo
A sound wave reflected back from a surface.
Reflected
Bounced back after meeting a surface.
Crust
The thin, solid, rocky outer layer of the Earth.
Mantle
The thick, mostly solid layer below the crust that can flow slowly.
Magma
Molten rock while it is underground.
commonMistakes: Thinking sound can travel through space.
Space is a vacuum with no particles.
Saying the Moon makes its own light.
The object in the middle is different.
Calling the mantle a liquid ocean.

realProblems.prompt: The adhan is called from a tall minaret in Hargeisa and is heard across the town. Explain how the sound reaches homes far away, using the idea of a sound wave and a medium.
realProblems.answer: 1 The vibrating voice/loudspeaker makes the air particles vibrate. The vibration passes from particle to particle as a sound wave through the air (the medium), spreading outward in all directions until it reaches the ears of people far away.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1 The vibrating voice/loudspeaker makes the air particles vibrate. The vibration passes from particle to particle as a sound wave through the air (the medium), spreading outward in all directions until it reaches the ears of people far away.

realProblems.prompt: Amina presses her ear to a wooden bench and hears a tap much more clearly than through the air. Explain why.
realProblems.answer: 2 Wood is a solid, and its particles are packed tightly together, so they pass the vibration along more efficiently than the loosely spaced particles in air. The sound is therefore clearer through the bench.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 2 Wood is a solid, and its particles are packed tightly together, so they pass the vibration along more efficiently than the loosely spaced particles in air. The sound is therefore clearer through the bench.

realProblems.prompt: A fisherman on the Indian Ocean coast uses an echo-sounder to find the depth of water. If the sound takes time to go down and come back, explain why he must remember the sound travels the distance twice.
realProblems.answer: 3 The sound must travel down to the seabed and then reflect back up to the boat, so it covers the distance twice. To find the true depth, the total distance must be halved.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 3 The sound must travel down to the seabed and then reflect back up to the boat, so it covers the distance twice. To find the true depth, the total distance must be halved.

realProblems.prompt: People living near the Great Rift Valley sometimes feel tremors and see volcanoes. Explain what is happening to the tectonic plates in that region.
realProblems.answer: 4 The African plate is slowly splitting along the Rift Valley. As the plates pull apart, magma rises to form volcanoes, and the shifting and sticking of the plates releases pressure as earthquakes/tremors.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 4 The African plate is slowly splitting along the Rift Valley. As the plates pull apart, magma rises to form volcanoes, and the shifting and sticking of the plates releases pressure as earthquakes/tremors.

realProblems.prompt: A family in Nairobi hears that a lunar eclipse will happen tonight. Explain why they can safely watch it, but must NEVER watch a solar eclipse directly.
realProblems.answer: 5 A lunar eclipse only involves the Earth's shadow falling on the Moon, so looking at the dim Moon is safe. A solar eclipse involves looking towards the Sun, whose light can permanently damage or blind the eyes, so it must never be viewed directly.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 5 A lunar eclipse only involves the Earth's shadow falling on the Moon, so looking at the dim Moon is safe. A solar eclipse involves looking towards the Sun, whose light can permanently damage or blind the eyes, so it must never be viewed directly.

realProblems.prompt: Yusuf shouts at a cliff and hears the echo 3 seconds later. Sound travels at 343 metres per second. How far away is the cliff? Show your working.
realProblems.answer: 1 distance = speed x time = 343 x 3 = 1029 metres. That is the round trip, so the cliff is 1029 / 2 = 514.5 metres away (about 515 m).
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1 distance = speed x time = 343 x 3 = 1029 metres. That is the round trip, so the cliff is 1029 / 2 = 514.5 metres away (about 515 m).

reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1 To vibrate means to move backwards and forwards very quickly, again and again. This movement is what makes sound.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Describe the difference between the loudness and the pitch of a sound.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 2 Loudness (volume) is how loud or quiet a sound is, and depends on the SIZE of the vibration. Pitch is how high or low a sound is, and depends on the SPEED of the vibration.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain, using the word 'particles', why sound cannot travel through a vacuum.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 3 Sound travels by making particles vibrate and pass the energy along. A vacuum has no particles, so there is nothing to vibrate and the wave cannot form or move.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: State what a medium is and give one example of a solid medium for sound.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 4 A medium is the substance a wave travels through. A solid example is wood (or a table, a string, the ground).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain why bats and fishing boats both use echoes.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 5 Both send out a sound and listen for the echo bouncing back off an object (an insect for a bat, the seabed or fish for a boat) to work out where it is or how far away it is.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the four layers of the Earth in order from the surface to the centre.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 6 From the surface to the centre: crust, mantle, outer core, inner core.

questions.question: What is needed for a sound to be made?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: A vibration is always needed to make a sound; heat, light and magnets do not make sound by themselves.

questions.question: A guitar makes a sound mainly because:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: It is the vibrating strings that make the note, not the wood or the metal parts.

questions.question: Sound travels through air as a:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Sound travels through air as a sound wave, passed particle to particle.

questions.question: Which of these can sound NOT travel through?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: A vacuum has no particles, so there is nothing to vibrate and carry the sound.

questions.question: The pitch of a sound depends on:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Pitch depends on how fast the object vibrates; faster vibration gives a higher pitch.

questions.question: A reflected sound wave is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: A reflected sound wave is called an echo.

questions.question: Which surface gives the BEST echo?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Hard, smooth, flat surfaces like glass reflect sound best; soft materials absorb it.

questions.question: The layers of the Earth from the outside inwards are:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: The correct order from outside to centre is crust, mantle, core.

questions.question: The inner core of the Earth is:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: The inner core is solid metal (iron and nickel), kept solid by huge pressure.

questions.question: Tectonic plates move at about the speed that:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Plates move only about 4 cm a year, about as fast as fingernails grow.

questions.question: An earthquake is caused when plates:
options: stick, build up pressure, then suddenly slip
questions.answer: stick, build up pressure, then suddenly slip
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Plates stick due to friction, pressure builds, then they suddenly slip: an earthquake.

questions.question: Molten rock on the surface of the Earth is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Molten rock is called lava on the surface and magma underground.

selfAssessment: I can explain that sound is made by vibrations and describe how those vibrations start.
I can describe how a sound wave travels by making particles vibrate backwards and forwards.
I can state that sound can travel through solids, liquids and gases, but not through a vacuum, and explain why.
I can describe how sound waves are reflected to make echoes, and give useful and unwanted examples.
I can distinguish between loudness (volume) and pitch, and connect each to the vibration.
I can describe the structure of the Earth: crust, mantle, outer core and inner core.
I can explain the evidence for continental drift and describe tectonic plates.
I can explain how fold mountains, volcanoes and earthquakes form at plate boundaries.
